intent: Check whether the candidate understands closures as lexical environment retention and can connect the concept to practical callback, factory, and state-hiding use cases.
primaryQuestion: What is a closure in JavaScript? Give a practical use case where closures are useful.

expectedSignals.label: Defines a closure as a function that keeps access to variables from its lexical scope after the outer function has finished.

requirements.description: Explains that a closure is the combination of a function and the lexical environment it was created in.
States that variables from the outer scope remain accessible later, even after the outer function has returned.

expectedSignals.label: Explains closure behavior without treating closed-over variables as global variables.

requirements.description: Clarifies that closure state belongs to a specific lexical scope, not the global object.
Mentions that closures retain access to variable bindings and can observe later changes to those bindings.

expectedSignals.label: Gives a practical use case such as function factories, private state, event handlers, timers, or callbacks.

requirements.description: Names at least one realistic closure use case rather than only giving a textbook definition.
Explains why retained lexical state is useful in that use case.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes a common risk such as accidentally sharing mutable state or retaining memory longer than expected.

requirements.description: Mentions that multiple closures can share the same mutable binding if created from the same scope.
Mentions that closed-over values can live longer than expected while references remain reachable.

scoringHints.description: Defines closure through lexical scope, gives a practical use case, and explains both benefit and a common bug or lifetime risk.
Understands that inner functions access outer variables but misses one nuance such as binding lifetime or shared mutable state.
Can repeat that closures are functions inside functions but cannot explain why the captured scope still matters.
Answer is too vague to distinguish closure from normal scope or global variables.

followUps.question: Can you show a tiny function factory example where each returned function remembers a different value?
followUps.purpose: Force the definition into observable closure behavior.

followUps.question: What can go wrong if many event handlers close over a large object that is no longer needed?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate understands closure lifetime and memory consequences.

en.title: Closures and retained lexical state
en.displayQuestion: What is a closure in JavaScript? Give a practical use case where closures are useful.
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can explain closures as retained lexical state, not just nested functions.
guidance: Define closure through lexical scope and retained variables.
Use a practical example such as a counter, event handler, or function factory.
Mention one common risk, such as shared mutable state or retained memory.
commonMistakes: Saying a closure is only a function inside another function.
Treating closed-over variables as globals.
Ignoring lifetime and shared state consequences.

vi.title: Closure và trạng thái lexical được giữ lại
vi.displayQuestion: Closure trong JavaScript là gì? Hãy nêu một use case thực tế nơi closure hữu ích.
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu closure là việc giữ lexical state, không chỉ là hàm lồng trong hàm.
guidance: Định nghĩa closure thông qua lexical scope và biến được giữ lại.
Dùng ví dụ thực tế như counter, event handler hoặc function factory.
Nêu một rủi ro như shared mutable state hoặc giữ bộ nhớ quá lâu.
commonMistakes: Nói closure chỉ là một hàm nằm trong hàm khác.
Xem biến được closure giữ lại như biến global.
Bỏ qua hệ quả về lifetime và shared state.

ja.title: クロージャと保持される lexical state
ja.displayQuestion: JavaScript の closure とは何ですか。closure が役立つ実用的な use case を挙げてください。
ja.displayIntent: closure を単なる入れ子関数ではなく、保持された lexical state として説明できるかを確認する。
guidance: lexical scope と保持される変数で closure を定義する。
counter、event handler、function factory などの実例を使う。
shared mutable state や memory retention などのリスクを一つ述べる。
commonMistakes: closure を関数の中の関数だけだと説明する。
閉じ込められた変数を global variable と混同する。
lifetime と shared state の影響を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript Guide: Closures
sourceReferences.note: Used for lexical environment and closure behavior.

sourceReferences.label: ECMAScript Language Specification
sourceReferences.note: Used as the canonical language semantics reference.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can reason about declaration creation, initialization timing, and why var, let, and const behave differently before their declaration line.
primaryQuestion: Explain hoisting. What is the difference in hoisting behavior between var, let, and const?

expectedSignals.label: Explains hoisting as declaration or binding setup during scope creation, not physical movement of code.

requirements.description: Explains that bindings are created before the code in the scope executes.
Avoids saying the JavaScript engine literally moves source lines to the top.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes var as function-scoped and initialized to undefined before assignment.

requirements.description: States that var is scoped to the nearest function or global scope.
Explains that reading var before assignment produces undefined instead of a temporal dead zone error.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that let and const are block-scoped and unavailable before initialization because of the temporal dead zone.

requirements.description: States that let and const are scoped to the nearest block.
Mentions that accessing let or const before initialization throws a ReferenceError.

expectedSignals.label: Connects the model to practical bug prevention in loops, conditional blocks, and refactors.

requirements.description: Uses a concrete loop or block example where scope choice changes behavior.
Explains why let and const make accidental early reads or scope leaks easier to catch.

scoringHints.description: Explains binding creation, var initialization, let/const TDZ, block versus function scope, and gives a concrete failure example.
Correctly describes the main var, let, and const differences but misses one execution timing nuance.
Knows the words hoisting and TDZ but cannot predict what a short code sample will do.
Answer is a memorized rule with no useful execution model.

followUps.question: What happens if console.log(user) runs before let user = 'Ava', and why?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate can apply TDZ rather than recite it.

followUps.question: Why can replacing var with let in a loop change callback behavior?
followUps.purpose: Connect hoisting and scope to a common production bug.

en.title: Hoisting, scope, and temporal dead zone
en.displayQuestion: Explain hoisting. What is the difference in hoisting behavior between var, let, and const?
en.displayIntent: Check declaration timing and scope fundamentals.
guidance: Explain binding setup before execution.
Compare var with let and const using a small example.
Mention temporal dead zone and ReferenceError.
commonMistakes: Saying source code is physically moved.
Saying let and const are not hoisted at all.
Forgetting block scope versus function scope.

vi.title: Hoisting, scope và temporal dead zone
vi.displayQuestion: Hãy giải thích hoisting. var, let và const khác nhau như thế nào về hoisting?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra nền tảng về thời điểm tạo binding và scope.
guidance: Giải thích việc setup binding trước khi thực thi.
So sánh var với let và const bằng ví dụ nhỏ.
Nhắc tới temporal dead zone và ReferenceError.
commonMistakes: Nói source code thật sự bị di chuyển.
Nói let và const hoàn toàn không được hoist.
Quên khác biệt giữa block scope và function scope.

ja.title: hoisting、scope、temporal dead zone
ja.displayQuestion: hoisting とは何ですか。var、let、const の hoisting の違いを説明してください。
ja.displayIntent: binding の作成タイミングと scope の基礎を確認する。
guidance: 実行前に binding が準備されることを説明する。
短い例で var と let/const を比較する。
temporal dead zone と ReferenceError に触れる。
commonMistakes: source code が物理的に移動すると説明する。
let と const は全く hoist されないと言う。
block scope と function scope の違いを忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript Guide: Grammar and types
sourceReferences.note: Used for variable declarations and scope behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript Guide: Closures
sourceReferences.note: Used for scope and lexical environment context.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can reason about this from call-site rules, lexical arrow behavior, class methods, and explicit binding APIs.
primaryQuestion: What does this refer to inside a regular function, an arrow function, and a class method? How do bind(), call(), and apply() affect this?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that regular function this is usually determined by how the function is called.

requirements.description: States that a regular function's this is based on call-site rules, such as obj.method() versus a detached function call.
Mentions that an unbound regular function call can have undefined this in strict mode or global object behavior in sloppy mode.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that arrow functions do not create their own this and instead capture this lexically.

requirements.description: States that arrow functions use this from the surrounding lexical scope.
Recognizes that call, apply, and bind do not change an arrow function's lexical this.

expectedSignals.label: Describes class methods as normal functions whose this depends on the receiver unless bound.

requirements.description: Explains that obj.method() or instance.method() sets this to the receiver object.
Mentions that passing a method as a callback can lose the original receiver unless it is bound or wrapped.

expectedSignals.label: Compares bind, call, and apply accurately.

requirements.description: Explains that bind returns a new function with this preset.
Explains that call and apply invoke immediately, with call taking arguments individually and apply taking an array-like argument list.

scoringHints.description: Uses call-site rules, lexical arrow behavior, detached method examples, and correctly compares bind, call, and apply.
Gets the main regular versus arrow function distinction but misses one nuance such as detached class methods.
Relies on broad statements like this means the current object without being able to explain callback behavior.
Cannot predict this in common function and method examples.

followUps.question: If const fn = user.getName; fn() is called later, why might this no longer be user?
followUps.purpose: Check call-site reasoning with a detached method.

followUps.question: Why is an arrow function often useful inside a setTimeout callback inside a class method?
followUps.purpose: Check lexical this understanding in a practical callback scenario.

en.title: this binding in functions and methods
en.displayQuestion: What does this refer to inside a regular function, an arrow function, and a class method? How do bind(), call(), and apply() affect this?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate reasons about this from call-site and lexical binding rules.
guidance: Explain regular function this from how the function is called.
Explain arrow functions through lexical this.
Compare bind, call, and apply with callback examples.
commonMistakes: Saying this always means the object where the function was defined.
Forgetting detached methods lose their receiver.
Trying to rebind arrow function this.

vi.title: Binding của this trong function và method
vi.displayQuestion: this trỏ tới đâu trong regular function, arrow function và class method? bind(), call(), apply() ảnh hưởng tới this như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có suy luận this dựa trên call-site và lexical binding hay không.
guidance: Giải thích this của regular function dựa trên cách hàm được gọi.
Giải thích arrow function thông qua lexical this.
So sánh bind, call và apply bằng ví dụ callback.
commonMistakes: Nói this luôn là object nơi function được định nghĩa.
Quên rằng method bị tách ra sẽ mất receiver.
Cố rebind this của arrow function.

ja.title: function と method の this binding
ja.displayQuestion: regular function、arrow function、class method の中で this は何を指しますか。bind(), call(), apply() は this にどう影響しますか。
ja.displayIntent: this を call-site と lexical binding のルールから説明できるかを確認する。
guidance: regular function の this は呼び出し方で決まることを説明する。
arrow function は lexical this を使うことを説明する。
callback の例で bind、call、apply を比較する。
commonMistakes: this は function が定義された object を常に指すと言う。
detached method が receiver を失うことを忘れる。
arrow function の this を rebind できると思う。

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript Reference: this
sourceReferences.note: Used for this binding rules.

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript Reference: Arrow functions
sourceReferences.note: Used for lexical this behavior.

intent: Check whether the candidate can explain the JavaScript event loop well enough to predict ordering between synchronous code, promise microtasks, and timer callbacks.
primaryQuestion: Explain the JavaScript event loop. In what order do synchronous code, Promise microtasks, and setTimeout(..., 0) tasks run?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that synchronous code runs on the call stack first until the current turn finishes.

requirements.description: States that synchronous JavaScript runs to completion on the call stack before queued callbacks run.
Mentions the run-to-completion model or equivalent wording.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes tasks from microtasks and places Promise callbacks in the microtask queue.

requirements.description: States that Promise then/catch/finally callbacks run as microtasks.
States that setTimeout callback runs as a task, after the current stack and microtasks.

expectedSignals.label: Predicts the usual order: synchronous logs, microtasks, then timer tasks.

requirements.description: Correctly predicts synchronous output before promise microtask output.
Correctly predicts promise microtasks before setTimeout with zero delay.

expectedSignals.label: Connects event loop knowledge to UI responsiveness, async debugging, or Node.js latency.

requirements.description: Explains why blocking the stack or flooding microtasks can delay UI updates or request handling.
Names a practical debugging symptom such as logs appearing out of expected order or timers firing late.

scoringHints.description: Correctly explains stack, tasks, microtasks, run-to-completion, output order, and a practical debugging consequence.
Predicts common ordering but misses one detail such as task versus microtask terminology.
Knows async code runs later but cannot explain why Promise callbacks run before setTimeout.
Cannot reason about event ordering from a simple code sample.

followUps.question: Given console.log('A'); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('B')); setTimeout(() => console.log('C'), 0); console.log('D'); what is printed?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete ordering from the event loop model.

followUps.question: Why can a long synchronous loop make a timer or UI update feel delayed?
followUps.purpose: Connect event loop theory to responsiveness and debugging.

en.title: Event loop ordering
en.displayQuestion: Explain the JavaScript event loop. In what order do synchronous code, Promise microtasks, and setTimeout(..., 0) tasks run?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can predict async ordering from the event loop model.
guidance: Start with call stack and run-to-completion.
Separate task queue from microtask queue.
Use a short console.log example to prove the order.
commonMistakes: Saying setTimeout with zero delay runs immediately.
Putting Promise callbacks after timers.
Ignoring that long synchronous work blocks later callbacks.

vi.title: Thứ tự chạy của event loop
vi.displayQuestion: Hãy giải thích event loop trong JavaScript. Synchronous code, Promise microtasks và setTimeout(..., 0) chạy theo thứ tự nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có dự đoán được thứ tự async dựa trên event loop hay không.
guidance: Bắt đầu từ call stack và run-to-completion.
Tách task queue và microtask queue.
Dùng ví dụ console.log ngắn để chứng minh thứ tự.
commonMistakes: Nói setTimeout delay 0 chạy ngay lập tức.
Cho Promise callback chạy sau timer.
Bỏ qua việc synchronous work dài sẽ chặn callback phía sau.

ja.title: event loop の実行順序
ja.displayQuestion: JavaScript の event loop を説明してください。synchronous code、Promise microtasks、setTimeout(..., 0) tasks はどの順番で実行されますか。
ja.displayIntent: event loop model から async の実行順序を予測できるかを確認する。
guidance: call stack と run-to-completion から説明する。
task queue と microtask queue を分ける。
短い console.log の例で順序を示す。
commonMistakes: setTimeout 0 が即時に実行されると言う。
Promise callback を timer の後に置く。
長い synchronous work が後続 callback を遅らせることを無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript execution model
sourceReferences.note: Used for event loop and job queue explanation.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Promise
sourceReferences.note: Used for promise microtask behavior context.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands JavaScript object lookup through the prototype chain and can distinguish prototypal inheritance from class syntax.
primaryQuestion: What is the prototype chain? How does prototypal inheritance differ from classical class-based inheritance?

expectedSignals.label: Explains property lookup through an object's own properties and then its prototype chain.

requirements.description: States that JavaScript checks the object itself before walking up its prototype.
Explains that lookup continues through prototypes until the property is found or the chain ends.

expectedSignals.label: Connects methods on shared prototypes to memory and behavior sharing.

requirements.description: Explains that methods can be shared through a prototype rather than copied onto each instance.
Mentions that an own property can shadow a property with the same name on the prototype.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that JavaScript class syntax is primarily syntax over prototype-based behavior.

requirements.description: States that JavaScript classes still use prototypes underneath.
Avoids describing JavaScript classes as the same runtime model as Java or C# classes.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes practical implications such as shared methods, instanceof behavior, and avoiding prototype mutation surprises.

requirements.description: Mentions instanceof, constructor functions, or class instances as related to prototype linkage.
Mentions that mutating prototypes can affect many objects and should be done carefully.

scoringHints.description: Explains lookup order, prototype sharing, class syntax over prototypes, shadowing, and one practical implication.
Understands lookup through prototypes but misses class syntax or shadowing nuance.
Knows the term prototype but cannot explain what happens during property lookup.
Answer confuses prototypes with unrelated concepts such as modules or TypeScript interfaces.

followUps.question: If obj.name is missing on obj but exists on Object.getPrototypeOf(obj), what happens when obj.name is read?
followUps.purpose: Force a concrete property lookup explanation.

followUps.question: Why are methods usually placed on a prototype instead of being recreated for every object?
followUps.purpose: Check understanding of shared behavior and memory implications.

en.title: Prototype chain and inheritance
en.displayQuestion: What is the prototype chain? How does prototypal inheritance differ from classical class-based inheritance?
en.displayIntent: Check JavaScript object lookup and inheritance fundamentals.
guidance: Explain property lookup step by step.
Mention shared prototype methods and shadowing.
Clarify that class syntax still uses prototypes.
commonMistakes: Treating JavaScript classes as a different inheritance runtime.
Ignoring own property shadowing.
Thinking methods are copied into every instance by default.

vi.title: Prototype chain và inheritance
vi.displayQuestion: Prototype chain là gì? Prototypal inheritance khác classical class-based inheritance như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra nền tảng lookup object và inheritance trong JavaScript.
guidance: Giải thích từng bước khi đọc property.
Nhắc tới method dùng chung trên prototype và shadowing.
Làm rõ class syntax vẫn dùng prototype.
commonMistakes: Xem JavaScript class như một runtime inheritance khác hẳn.
Bỏ qua own property shadowing.
Nghĩ method mặc định được copy vào từng instance.

ja.title: prototype chain と inheritance
ja.displayQuestion: prototype chain とは何ですか。prototypal inheritance は classical class-based inheritance とどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: JavaScript の object lookup と inheritance の基礎を確認する。
guidance: property lookup を段階的に説明する。
prototype 上の共有 method と shadowing に触れる。
class syntax も prototype を使うことを明確にする。
commonMistakes: JavaScript class を別の inheritance runtime として扱う。
own property shadowing を無視する。
method が各 instance に copy されると思う。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Inheritance and the prototype chain
sourceReferences.note: Used for prototype chain and prototypal inheritance concepts.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Classes
sourceReferences.note: Used for class syntax over prototype-based behavior.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands equality and type coercion enough to avoid common production bugs in validation, form input, and defaulting logic.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between == and ===? What is type coercion, and when can it cause unexpected bugs?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that === compares without implicit type conversion while == may coerce operands before comparison.

requirements.description: States that strict equality checks value and type without implicit conversion.
States that loose equality can convert one or both operands before comparing.

expectedSignals.label: Gives realistic bug examples involving strings from forms, numbers, booleans, null, or undefined.

requirements.description: Mentions that data from forms, query strings, localStorage, or JSON may be strings even when it looks numeric.
Explains how implicit conversion can make a condition pass or fail unexpectedly.

expectedSignals.label: Understands operator coercion examples such as string concatenation with + and numeric conversion with -, *, or unary +.

requirements.description: Explains that + can concatenate when a string operand is involved.
Explains that -, *, and unary + typically coerce operands toward numbers.

expectedSignals.label: States a safe practice: prefer explicit conversion and strict comparison except for intentional narrow cases.

requirements.description: Recommends converting input intentionally with Number, Boolean, parsing, or validation before comparing.
Recommends strict equality as the default team convention unless loose equality is deliberate and documented.

scoringHints.description: Explains equality, operator coercion, real data-boundary bugs, and safe explicit conversion practices.
Understands == versus === but misses operator coercion or a realistic bug example.
Only says always use triple equals without explaining what coercion would have done.
Cannot explain simple examples such as '37' + 7 versus '37' - 7.

followUps.question: What do '37' + 7 and '37' - 7 return, and why are they different?
followUps.purpose: Check practical operator coercion knowledge.

followUps.question: If an API query parameter page='0' is checked with if (page), what bug might happen?
followUps.purpose: Connect coercion and truthiness to real input handling.

en.title: Equality and coercion bugs
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between == and ===? What is type coercion, and when can it cause unexpected bugs?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can reason about implicit conversion instead of only memorizing equality rules.
guidance: Explain strict versus loose equality.
Use examples with form or query string input.
Mention explicit conversion before comparison.
commonMistakes: Only saying use === without explaining why.
Forgetting that + can concatenate strings.
Ignoring null, undefined, and input-boundary cases.

vi.title: Equality và lỗi coercion
vi.displayQuestion: == và === khác nhau như thế nào? Type coercion là gì và khi nào nó gây bug bất ngờ?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có suy luận implicit conversion thay vì chỉ học thuộc quy tắc equality hay không.
guidance: Giải thích strict equality và loose equality.
Dùng ví dụ với input từ form hoặc query string.
Nhắc tới việc convert rõ ràng trước khi so sánh.
commonMistakes: Chỉ nói dùng === mà không giải thích lý do.
Quên rằng + có thể nối chuỗi.
Bỏ qua null, undefined và dữ liệu ở boundary.

ja.title: equality と coercion のバグ
ja.displayQuestion: == と === は何が違いますか。type coercion とは何で、どのような時に予期しない bug を起こしますか。
ja.displayIntent: equality の暗記ではなく implicit conversion を reasoning できるかを確認する。
guidance: strict equality と loose equality を説明する。
form input や query string の例を使う。
比較前に明示的に変換することに触れる。
commonMistakes: なぜかを説明せずに === を使うと言うだけ。
+ が string concatenation になり得ることを忘れる。
null、undefined、boundary data を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Equality comparisons and sameness
sourceReferences.note: Used for equality comparison behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Type coercion glossary
sourceReferences.note: Used for coercion terminology.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands how single-threaded JavaScript coordinates asynchronous work through runtime APIs and queued callbacks without blocking the main execution thread.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous code? How does JavaScript, being single-threaded, handle async operations?

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes synchronous code that blocks the current execution path from asynchronous code that completes later.

requirements.description: Explains that synchronous code runs immediately in order and blocks the current stack until it finishes.
Explains that asynchronous work lets the program continue and reports completion later.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that JavaScript execution is single-threaded, but browser or Node.js runtime APIs can perform work outside the JS call stack.

requirements.description: States that normal JavaScript code runs on one main call stack per execution context.
Mentions runtime APIs such as timers, network I/O, DOM events, or Node.js APIs as sources of asynchronous completion.

expectedSignals.label: Connects async completion back to callbacks, promises, or async/await through the event loop.

requirements.description: Explains that callbacks or promise reactions run later when the async operation settles.
Mentions the event loop as the mechanism that schedules later JavaScript work.

expectedSignals.label: Understands that async does not make CPU-heavy JavaScript automatically parallel.

requirements.description: States that long CPU-bound JavaScript still blocks the thread unless moved to a worker or separate process.
Distinguishes non-blocking I/O from parallel CPU execution.

scoringHints.description: Explains sync versus async flow, single JS stack, runtime-managed I/O/timers, event loop scheduling, and CPU-bound limits.
Understands async completion but misses one nuance such as CPU-bound blocking or runtime involvement.
Thinks async means JavaScript simply runs everything in parallel.
Cannot explain why async callbacks run later while code after the async call continues.

followUps.question: If fetch starts and the next console.log runs immediately, what is JavaScript doing while the network request is pending?
followUps.purpose: Check runtime and event loop understanding.

followUps.question: Would wrapping a CPU-heavy loop in an async function stop it from blocking the page? Why or why not?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate distinguishes async syntax from parallel execution.

en.title: Synchronous versus asynchronous JavaScript
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous code? How does JavaScript, being single-threaded, handle async operations?
en.displayIntent: Check the candidate's mental model for non-blocking JavaScript.
guidance: Separate JavaScript execution from browser or Node.js runtime work.
Mention callbacks, promises, or async/await as continuation mechanisms.
Clarify that async I/O is not the same as parallel CPU execution.
commonMistakes: Saying async automatically creates a new JavaScript thread.
Ignoring the role of the runtime and event loop.
Thinking async functions make CPU-heavy code non-blocking.

vi.title: JavaScript synchronous và asynchronous
vi.displayQuestion: Synchronous code và asynchronous code khác nhau như thế nào? JavaScript single-threaded xử lý async operation ra sao?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra mental model của ứng viên về JavaScript non-blocking.
guidance: Tách việc thực thi JavaScript khỏi công việc của browser hoặc Node.js runtime.
Nhắc tới callback, promise hoặc async/await như cơ chế tiếp tục xử lý.
Làm rõ async I/O không đồng nghĩa với chạy CPU song song.
commonMistakes: Nói async tự tạo JavaScript thread mới.
Bỏ qua vai trò của runtime và event loop.
Nghĩ async function làm CPU-heavy code hết blocking.

ja.title: synchronous と asynchronous JavaScript
ja.displayQuestion: synchronous code と asynchronous code は何が違いますか。single-threaded な JavaScript は async operation をどう扱いますか。
ja.displayIntent: non-blocking JavaScript の mental model を確認する。
guidance: JavaScript execution と browser/Node.js runtime の仕事を分ける。
callback、promise、async/await を continuation mechanism として説明する。
async I/O と parallel CPU execution は同じではないと明確にする。
commonMistakes: async が自動的に新しい JavaScript thread を作ると言う。
runtime と event loop の役割を無視する。
async function が CPU-heavy code を non-blocking にすると考える。

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript execution model
sourceReferences.note: Used for event loop and run-to-completion concepts.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Asynchronous JavaScript
sourceReferences.note: Used for asynchronous programming concepts.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands browser event propagation and can use event delegation for dynamic lists without attaching unnecessary listeners.
primaryQuestion: What is event delegation? Why is it preferred over attaching event listeners to individual elements, and what is the difference between event.target and event.currentTarget?

expectedSignals.label: Defines event delegation as listening on an ancestor and handling events from matching descendants.

requirements.description: States that the listener is attached to a parent or ancestor element.
Explains that the handler checks the actual event source or closest matching descendant.

expectedSignals.label: Explains the role of bubbling in making delegation work.

requirements.description: Mentions that many DOM events bubble from the target up through ancestors.
Recognizes that not every event bubbles or that capture/listener options may matter.

expectedSignals.label: Compares event.target and event.currentTarget accurately.

requirements.description: Explains that event.target is the actual element that initiated the event or the deepest relevant target.
Explains that event.currentTarget is the element whose listener is currently running.

expectedSignals.label: Names practical benefits and precautions: dynamic elements, fewer listeners, filtering, and not acting on the wrong child.

requirements.description: Explains that delegation helps with dynamic lists or many child elements without one listener per item.
Mentions filtering with matches, closest, dataset, or containment checks to avoid handling the wrong element.

scoringHints.description: Explains delegation through bubbling, target/currentTarget, dynamic-list benefits, and safe target filtering.
Understands parent listener delegation but misses one nuance such as currentTarget or filtering.
Knows delegation means fewer listeners but cannot explain propagation or target behavior.
Cannot distinguish the clicked element from the element where the listener was registered.

followUps.question: In a ul click listener, what are event.target and event.currentTarget when the user clicks a button inside an li?
followUps.purpose: Force a concrete target/currentTarget explanation.

followUps.question: What check would you add so a parent listener only handles clicks on delete buttons?
followUps.purpose: Check safe filtering and practical implementation.

en.title: Event delegation and DOM targets
en.displayQuestion: What is event delegation? Why is it preferred over attaching event listeners to individual elements, and what is the difference between event.target and event.currentTarget?
en.displayIntent: Check browser event propagation and practical DOM event handling.
guidance: Explain bubbling and ancestor listeners.
Compare target and currentTarget.
Mention filtering for matching descendants.
commonMistakes: Thinking target and currentTarget are always the same.
Forgetting dynamic elements.
Handling every child click without filtering.

vi.title: Event delegation và DOM target
vi.displayQuestion: Event delegation là gì? Vì sao nó thường tốt hơn việc gắn listener cho từng element, và event.target khác event.currentTarget như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về event propagation và xử lý DOM event thực tế.
guidance: Giải thích bubbling và listener ở ancestor.
So sánh target với currentTarget.
Nhắc tới việc filter descendant phù hợp.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ target và currentTarget luôn giống nhau.
Quên case element được thêm động.
Xử lý mọi click con mà không filter.

ja.title: event delegation と DOM target
ja.displayQuestion: event delegation とは何ですか。個別の element に listener を付けるより好まれる理由と、event.target と event.currentTarget の違いを説明してください。
ja.displayIntent: browser event propagation と実践的な DOM event handling を確認する。
guidance: bubbling と ancestor listener を説明する。
target と currentTarget を比較する。
対象 descendant を filter することに触れる。
commonMistakes: target と currentTarget が常に同じだと思う。
dynamic element の case を忘れる。
filter せずにすべての child click を処理する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Event bubbling
sourceReferences.note: Used for event bubbling and delegation concepts.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Event.target
sourceReferences.note: Used for target/currentTarget distinction.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands primitive values, objects, nullish values, and truthiness well enough to write safe conditionals and defaults.
primaryQuestion: What are JavaScript primitive types, how are primitives different from objects, and how do null, undefined, truthy, and falsy values affect conditions?

expectedSignals.label: Lists the common primitive types and separates primitives from objects.

requirements.description: Names string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, and null as primitive values in JavaScript terminology.
Explains that objects, arrays, and functions are reference-like objects rather than primitive values.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that primitives are immutable values while objects can have mutable properties.

requirements.description: States that primitive values themselves are immutable.
States that object properties can usually be mutated through references to the object.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes null from undefined in intent and data modeling.

requirements.description: Explains undefined as a missing, not-provided, or uninitialized value.
Explains null as an intentional empty or no-value marker when the code or API chooses to use it.

expectedSignals.label: Names common falsy values and connects truthiness to safe conditionals and defaults.

requirements.description: Names false, 0, -0, 0n, empty string, null, undefined, and NaN as common falsy values.
Explains that using truthiness for defaults can accidentally replace valid values like 0 or empty string.

scoringHints.description: Covers primitives, objects, mutability, null versus undefined, falsy values, and defaulting pitfalls.
Understands most value categories but misses one nuance such as NaN falsiness or intentional null.
Can list some types but cannot explain how value categories affect code behavior.
Answer mixes primitives, arrays, objects, null, and undefined without a coherent model.

followUps.question: Why is typeof [] not a good way to check whether a value is an array?
followUps.purpose: Check separation between objects, arrays, and primitive type checks.

followUps.question: If pageSize can validly be 0, why can pageSize || 20 be a bug?
followUps.purpose: Connect truthiness to defaulting mistakes.

en.title: Values, nullish state, and truthiness
en.displayQuestion: What are JavaScript primitive types, how are primitives different from objects, and how do null, undefined, truthy, and falsy values affect conditions?
en.displayIntent: Check the candidate's value model for safe conditionals and defaults.
guidance: Separate primitive values from objects.
Explain null versus undefined.
Name falsy values and defaulting pitfalls.
commonMistakes: Calling arrays primitive values.
Treating null and undefined as always interchangeable.
Using truthiness when 0 or empty string are valid values.

vi.title: Value, nullish state và truthiness
vi.displayQuestion: Primitive types trong JavaScript là gì, primitive khác object như thế nào, và null, undefined, truthy, falsy ảnh hưởng tới condition ra sao?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra model về value để viết condition và default an toàn.
guidance: Tách primitive value khỏi object.
Giải thích null và undefined.
Nêu falsy values và lỗi defaulting.
commonMistakes: Gọi array là primitive value.
Xem null và undefined luôn thay thế được cho nhau.
Dùng truthiness khi 0 hoặc chuỗi rỗng là giá trị hợp lệ.

ja.title: value、nullish state、truthiness
ja.displayQuestion: JavaScript の primitive types とは何ですか。primitive と object はどう違い、null、undefined、truthy、falsy は condition にどう影響しますか。
ja.displayIntent: 安全な condition と default のための value model を確認する。
guidance: primitive value と object を分ける。
null と undefined を説明する。
falsy values と defaulting の落とし穴を述べる。
commonMistakes: array を primitive value と呼ぶ。
null と undefined を常に同じものとして扱う。
0 や empty string が有効な値なのに truthiness を使う。

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript data types and data structures
sourceReferences.note: Used for primitive and object value categories.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Truthy
sourceReferences.note: Used for truthy/falsy conditional behavior.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands const as binding immutability rather than deep value immutability, especially for arrays and objects.
primaryQuestion: Why can a const object or array still be mutated? What exactly does const protect?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that const prevents rebinding the variable name to a different value.

requirements.description: States that assigning a new value to the const binding is not allowed.
Uses binding or variable reference language rather than saying const makes the value immutable.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that object and array contents can still be changed through the same reference.

requirements.description: States that properties of an object stored in const can usually be mutated.
States that arrays declared with const can still be pushed to, sorted, or have elements changed.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes shallow prevention techniques from deep immutability.

requirements.description: Mentions Object.freeze or equivalent as a way to prevent some direct mutations, with shallow limitations.
Explains that nested objects require additional handling for deep immutability.

expectedSignals.label: Connects the concept to safer state updates in application code.

requirements.description: Mentions creating a new object or array when immutable update semantics are desired.
Explains that mutating shared objects can create unexpected side effects.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates binding immutability from object mutation, mentions arrays, shallow freeze limits, and safe update patterns.
Understands const prevents reassignment but misses freeze depth or shared mutation consequences.
Knows const cannot be reassigned but incorrectly says objects are fully immutable.
Cannot explain why const user = {}; user.name = 'Ava' is allowed.

followUps.question: Is const user = {}; user.name = 'Ava' allowed? Is user = {} allowed? Why?
followUps.purpose: Force the distinction between mutation and rebinding.

followUps.question: Why can mutating a shared object be risky in UI state or request-handling code?
followUps.purpose: Check practical consequence beyond syntax.

en.title: const binding versus object mutation
en.displayQuestion: Why can a const object or array still be mutated? What exactly does const protect?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate separates variable binding from object mutability.
guidance: Explain const as protecting the binding.
Show object property or array mutation examples.
Mention immutable update patterns when mutation is unsafe.
commonMistakes: Saying const makes objects deeply immutable.
Confusing reassignment with mutation.
Ignoring shared reference side effects.

vi.title: const binding và object mutation
vi.displayQuestion: Vì sao const object hoặc array vẫn có thể bị mutate? const thật sự bảo vệ điều gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có tách được variable binding khỏi object mutability hay không.
guidance: Giải thích const bảo vệ binding.
Đưa ví dụ mutate property hoặc array.
Nhắc tới immutable update pattern khi mutation không an toàn.
commonMistakes: Nói const làm object immutable sâu.
Nhầm reassignment với mutation.
Bỏ qua side effect từ shared reference.

ja.title: const binding と object mutation
ja.displayQuestion: const の object や array が mutate できるのはなぜですか。const は正確には何を守りますか。
ja.displayIntent: variable binding と object mutability を分けて説明できるかを確認する。
guidance: const は binding を守ることを説明する。
object property や array mutation の例を示す。
mutation が危険な時の immutable update pattern に触れる。
commonMistakes: const が object を深く immutable にすると言う。
reassignment と mutation を混同する。
shared reference の side effect を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN const
sourceReferences.note: Used for const binding behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Object.freeze
sourceReferences.note: Used for shallow freezing and object immutability discussion.

intent: Check whether the candidate can choose between function declarations, function expressions, and arrow functions based on hoisting, this, arguments, and constructor behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between a function declaration, a function expression, and an arrow function?

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes declarations from expressions by naming, assignment, and hoisting behavior.

requirements.description: Explains that function declarations are generally available throughout their scope before their source position.
Explains that function expressions are values assigned to variables or passed around, with access depending on the variable declaration.

expectedSignals.label: Explains arrow functions as concise function expressions with lexical this.

requirements.description: States that an arrow function is an expression, commonly used as a callback or small function value.
Explains that arrow functions capture this from the surrounding scope.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions arguments and constructor differences for arrow functions.

requirements.description: Mentions that arrow functions do not have their own arguments object and rest parameters are preferred.
Mentions that arrow functions cannot be used as constructors with new.

expectedSignals.label: Chooses a function form based on readability, callbacks, methods, and team conventions.

requirements.description: Explains why a declaration can be clearer for named reusable functions.
Explains why a regular method/function is better when dynamic this or construction is required.

scoringHints.description: Compares declaration, expression, and arrow function behavior across hoisting, this, arguments, constructors, and practical selection.
Explains the main forms but misses one important arrow-function limitation.
Treats arrow functions as only shorter syntax without understanding behavioral differences.
Cannot tell which forms are hoisted or how this differs.

followUps.question: Why might an arrow function be a bad choice for an object method that needs this to point at the object?
followUps.purpose: Check practical lexical this consequences.

followUps.question: When would you prefer a named function declaration over an inline arrow callback?
followUps.purpose: Probe readability and debugging judgment, not only syntax recall.

en.title: Function declarations, expressions, and arrows
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between a function declaration, a function expression, and an arrow function?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands function forms beyond syntax.
guidance: Compare hoisting and assignment behavior.
Explain lexical this in arrow functions.
Mention arguments and constructor limitations.
commonMistakes: Saying arrow functions are only shorter functions.
Ignoring this and arguments behavior.
Using arrows for methods that require dynamic this.

vi.title: Function declaration, expression và arrow
vi.displayQuestion: Function declaration, function expression và arrow function khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên hiểu function form vượt khỏi cú pháp viết ngắn.
guidance: So sánh hoisting và hành vi assignment.
Giải thích lexical this trong arrow function.
Nhắc tới arguments và giới hạn constructor.
commonMistakes: Nói arrow function chỉ là function viết ngắn hơn.
Bỏ qua this và arguments behavior.
Dùng arrow cho method cần dynamic this.

ja.title: function declaration、expression、arrow
ja.displayQuestion: function declaration、function expression、arrow function は何が違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: 短い syntax だけでなく function form の振る舞いを理解しているかを確認する。
guidance: hoisting と assignment behavior を比較する。
arrow function の lexical this を説明する。
arguments と constructor の制限に触れる。
commonMistakes: arrow function は短い function だけだと言う。
this と arguments behavior を無視する。
dynamic this が必要な method に arrow を使う。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Functions
sourceReferences.note: Used for function declarations and expressions.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Arrow function expressions
sourceReferences.note: Used for arrow function behavior.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can reason about function arguments, primitive values, object references, and mutation side effects without using imprecise pass-by-reference language.
primaryQuestion: How does JavaScript pass arguments to functions? Explain primitive values versus object-reference behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that function parameters receive values, and an object value is a reference to an object.

requirements.description: Explains that JavaScript passes values into parameters.
Explains that the value for an object is a reference-like handle to the same object.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes reassignment of a parameter from mutating the object it refers to.

requirements.description: States that assigning parameter = newValue does not reassign the caller's variable.
States that changing a property on the object can be visible to other references to that object.

expectedSignals.label: Explains primitive behavior: changing a parameter holding a primitive does not change the original variable.

requirements.description: Explains that reassigning a primitive parameter only changes the local parameter binding.
Can illustrate with a small number or string example.

expectedSignals.label: Connects the model to defensive copying, pure functions, or avoiding hidden mutation bugs.

requirements.description: Mentions copying or cloning before mutation when callers should not observe changes.
Explains that mutating input objects creates side effects that callers may not expect.

scoringHints.description: Accurately explains parameter values, object reference values, reassignment versus mutation, primitives, and side-effect prevention.
Understands object mutation visibility but uses slightly imprecise wording or misses defensive copying.
Says primitives pass by value and objects pass by reference but cannot predict reassignment versus mutation behavior.
Cannot explain whether a function mutating obj.name affects the caller's object.

followUps.question: If a function does user.name = 'Ava', does the caller see it? What if the function does user = {}?
followUps.purpose: Force distinction between object mutation and parameter reassignment.

followUps.question: When would you copy an object before changing it inside a function?
followUps.purpose: Connect argument behavior to API design and side-effect prevention.

en.title: Arguments, references, and mutation
en.displayQuestion: How does JavaScript pass arguments to functions? Explain primitive values versus object-reference behavior.
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can reason about mutation side effects through function parameters.
guidance: Separate parameter reassignment from object mutation.
Use one primitive example and one object example.
Mention copying when mutation should not escape.
commonMistakes: Using pass-by-reference wording without explaining behavior.
Thinking parameter reassignment changes the caller's variable.
Ignoring hidden side effects from mutating input objects.

vi.title: Argument, reference và mutation
vi.displayQuestion: JavaScript truyền argument vào function như thế nào? Hãy giải thích primitive value so với hành vi object-reference.
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có suy luận được side effect khi mutate qua function parameter hay không.
guidance: Tách parameter reassignment khỏi object mutation.
Dùng một ví dụ primitive và một ví dụ object.
Nhắc tới copy khi mutation không nên thoát ra ngoài.
commonMistakes: Dùng pass-by-reference nhưng không giải thích hành vi.
Nghĩ parameter reassignment đổi biến của caller.
Bỏ qua side effect khi mutate input object.

ja.title: arguments、references、mutation
ja.displayQuestion: JavaScript は function に arguments をどのように渡しますか。primitive value と object-reference behavior を説明してください。
ja.displayIntent: function parameter 経由の mutation side effect を reasoning できるかを確認する。
guidance: parameter reassignment と object mutation を分ける。
primitive の例と object の例を一つずつ使う。
mutation を外に出したくない時の copy に触れる。
commonMistakes: behavior を説明せずに pass-by-reference とだけ言う。
parameter reassignment が caller の variable を変えると思う。
input object mutation の hidden side effect を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Functions
sourceReferences.note: Used for parameter and function behavior context.

sourceReferences.label: MDN JavaScript data types and data structures
sourceReferences.note: Used for primitive and object value categories.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands array iteration methods as data-flow tools and can choose the right one based on whether they transform, filter, reduce, or perform side effects.
primaryQuestion: Compare for...of, for...in, forEach(), map(), filter(), and reduce(). When should you use each?

expectedSignals.label: Separates array value iteration from object key/property iteration.

requirements.description: Explains that for...of iterates values from iterable objects such as arrays.
Explains that for...in iterates enumerable property keys and is usually not the first choice for arrays.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes forEach from map by side effects versus returning a transformed array.

requirements.description: States that forEach is for running a function for each item and returns undefined.
States that map returns a new array of transformed values.

expectedSignals.label: Explains filter as selecting items and reduce as accumulating a single result or structured aggregate.

requirements.description: Explains that filter returns items that pass a predicate.
Explains that reduce uses an accumulator to derive a result such as sum, map-by-id, or grouped data.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions readability and choosing simple loops when transformation methods become too clever.

requirements.description: Chooses the method based on the intended operation rather than habit.
Mentions that a clear for...of loop can be better than an overly complex reduce.

scoringHints.description: Accurately maps each iteration option to intent, return behavior, side effects, and readability trade-offs.
Correctly distinguishes map, filter, and forEach but misses for...in or reduce readability nuance.
Knows names of methods but chooses them randomly or cannot explain return values.
Cannot tell which method returns a new array or when for...in is inappropriate for arrays.

followUps.question: If you need an array of user names from an array of users, why is map a better signal than forEach?
followUps.purpose: Check method intent and return behavior.

followUps.question: When would you replace a reduce with a plain for...of loop?
followUps.purpose: Probe practical readability judgment.

en.title: Choosing array iteration tools
en.displayQuestion: Compare for...of, for...in, forEach(), map(), filter(), and reduce(). When should you use each?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate chooses iteration APIs by intent, not syntax habit.
guidance: Separate values, keys, side effects, transforms, filters, and accumulation.
Mention what returns a new array.
Discuss readability when reduce becomes too clever.
commonMistakes: Using for...in as the default array loop.
Using map only for side effects.
Writing hard-to-read reduce logic for simple loops.

vi.title: Chọn công cụ iterate array
vi.displayQuestion: Hãy so sánh for...of, for...in, forEach(), map(), filter(), reduce(). Khi nào nên dùng từng loại?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên chọn API iterate theo intent, không chỉ theo thói quen cú pháp.
guidance: Tách value, key, side effect, transform, filter và accumulation.
Nhắc rõ method nào trả về array mới.
Bàn về readability khi reduce quá khó đọc.
commonMistakes: Dùng for...in làm loop mặc định cho array.
Dùng map chỉ để tạo side effect.
Viết reduce khó đọc cho logic có thể là loop đơn giản.

ja.title: array iteration tool の選び方
ja.displayQuestion: for...of、for...in、forEach()、map()、filter()、reduce() を比較してください。それぞれいつ使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: syntax の癖ではなく intent によって iteration API を選べるかを確認する。
guidance: values、keys、side effects、transforms、filters、accumulation を分ける。
新しい array を返す method を明確にする。
reduce が複雑すぎる時の readability を話す。
commonMistakes: array loop の default として for...in を使う。
side effect だけのために map を使う。
単純な loop で済む処理を読みにくい reduce にする。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Array
sourceReferences.note: Used for array iteration methods.

sourceReferences.label: MDN for...of
sourceReferences.note: Used for iterable value iteration behavior.

intent: Check whether the candidate can reason about shallow copies, nested references, and mutation bugs when using object spread or Object.assign.
primaryQuestion: What is a shallow copy? Compare object spread ({ ...obj }) and Object.assign(). What happens with nested objects?

expectedSignals.label: Defines shallow copy as copying top-level properties while nested objects remain shared references.

requirements.description: Explains that a shallow copy creates a new outer object or array with top-level entries copied.
Explains that nested objects or arrays are still shared references.

expectedSignals.label: Compares object spread and Object.assign as common shallow-copy tools.

requirements.description: States that object spread performs a shallow copy of enumerable own properties into a new object.
States that Object.assign copies enumerable own properties into a target object and is shallow.

expectedSignals.label: Predicts mutation behavior for nested objects after a shallow copy.

requirements.description: Explains that changing copy.profile.name can affect original.profile.name if profile is shared.
Explains that replacing copy.profile with a new object changes the copy's top-level reference only.

expectedSignals.label: Names appropriate approaches when deep independence is needed.

requirements.description: Mentions structuredClone, library helpers, or explicit cloning of the nested parts that need independence.
Explains that deep cloning has trade-offs and should match the data shape and mutation needs.

scoringHints.description: Explains shallow copy mechanics, spread versus Object.assign, nested reference behavior, and when to use explicit or structured cloning.
Understands nested references stay shared but misses one tool or trade-off detail.
Thinks object spread creates a fully independent deep copy.
Cannot predict whether mutating a nested object affects the original after copying.

followUps.question: If const copy = { ...user } and copy.address.city is changed, when would user.address.city also change?
followUps.purpose: Force nested reference reasoning.

followUps.question: When would structuredClone be reasonable, and when would you clone only one nested branch instead?
followUps.purpose: Check practical clone strategy judgment.

en.title: Shallow copy and nested references
en.displayQuestion: What is a shallow copy? Compare object spread ({ ...obj }) and Object.assign(). What happens with nested objects?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can debug mutation caused by shared nested references.
guidance: Define top-level copy versus nested shared references.
Compare object spread and Object.assign.
Mention structuredClone or explicit nested copying when needed.
commonMistakes: Assuming spread is a deep clone.
Forgetting nested arrays or objects remain shared.
Deep cloning everything without considering cost or data shape.

vi.title: Shallow copy và nested reference
vi.displayQuestion: Shallow copy là gì? Hãy so sánh object spread ({ ...obj }) và Object.assign(). Chuyện gì xảy ra với nested object?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có debug được mutation do nested reference dùng chung hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa top-level copy so với nested reference dùng chung.
So sánh object spread và Object.assign.
Nhắc tới structuredClone hoặc copy nested branch khi cần.
commonMistakes: Tưởng spread là deep clone.
Quên nested array hoặc object vẫn dùng chung.
Deep clone mọi thứ mà không cân nhắc cost hoặc data shape.

ja.title: shallow copy と nested references
ja.displayQuestion: shallow copy とは何ですか。object spread ({ ...obj }) と Object.assign() を比較し、nested objects では何が起きるか説明してください。
ja.displayIntent: shared nested references による mutation bug を debug できるかを確認する。
guidance: top-level copy と shared nested reference を定義する。
object spread と Object.assign を比較する。
必要な時の structuredClone や explicit nested copy に触れる。
commonMistakes: spread を deep clone だと思う。
nested array や object が共有されたままだと忘れる。
cost や data shape を考えずにすべて deep clone する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Spread syntax
sourceReferences.note: Used for object and array spread behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MDN Object.assign
sourceReferences.note: Used for shallow assignment behavior.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands promises, async/await, error propagation, and why returning or awaiting async work prevents floating promise bugs.
primaryQuestion: Explain the three Promise states. How do then(), catch(), finally(), and async/await relate to promises, and what is a floating promise?

expectedSignals.label: Explains the three promise states: pending, fulfilled, and rejected.

requirements.description: Defines pending as not yet settled.
Defines fulfilled as successful settlement with a value and rejected as failed settlement with a reason.

expectedSignals.label: Explains then, catch, and finally as chainable reactions to promise settlement.

requirements.description: Explains that then handles fulfillment and can return a value or another promise for chaining.
Explains that catch handles rejection and finally runs cleanup regardless of fulfillment or rejection.

expectedSignals.label: Connects async/await to promises and error handling with try/catch.

requirements.description: States that an async function returns a promise and await pauses the async function until settlement.
Explains that try/catch can handle rejected promises when using await.

expectedSignals.label: Explains floating promises and why returning or awaiting promises matters.

requirements.description: Defines a floating promise as async work started without being returned, awaited, or otherwise observed.
Explains that floating promises can cause lost errors, premature completion, or out-of-order side effects.

scoringHints.description: Explains promise states, chaining, error propagation, async/await equivalence, try/catch, and floating promise consequences.
Understands promises and async/await but misses one practical bug such as not returning a promise from then.
Can use async/await syntax but cannot explain rejection flow or floating promises.
Cannot describe what happens when an awaited promise rejects.

followUps.question: Inside a then callback, why does returning fetchProfile(user.id) matter for the next then in the chain?
followUps.purpose: Check promise chaining and floating promise understanding.

followUps.question: What bug can happen if an async save() is called but not awaited before showing a success message?
followUps.purpose: Connect floating promises to user-visible ordering and error bugs.

en.title: Promises, async/await, and floating work
en.displayQuestion: Explain the three Promise states. How do then(), catch(), finally(), and async/await relate to promises, and what is a floating promise?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can reason about async completion and error propagation.
guidance: Define pending, fulfilled, and rejected.
Explain chaining and returning promises from then.
Connect async/await to promises and try/catch.
Describe floating promise risks.
commonMistakes: Thinking async/await is not based on promises.
Not returning promises from then callbacks.
Showing success before async work has actually finished.

vi.title: Promise, async/await và async work bị thả nổi
vi.displayQuestion: Hãy giải thích ba trạng thái của Promise. then(), catch(), finally() và async/await liên quan tới promise như thế nào, và floating promise là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có suy luận được async completion và error propagation hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa pending, fulfilled và rejected.
Giải thích chaining và việc return promise trong then.
Kết nối async/await với promise và try/catch.
Mô tả rủi ro của floating promise.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ async/await không dựa trên promise.
Không return promise từ then callback.
Hiển thị success trước khi async work thật sự hoàn tất.

ja.title: promises、async/await、floating work
ja.displayQuestion: Promise の三つの状態を説明してください。then(), catch(), finally(), async/await は promise とどう関係し、floating promise とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: async completion と error propagation を reasoning できるかを確認する。
guidance: pending、fulfilled、rejected を定義する。
chaining と then から promise を return する意味を説明する。
async/await と promise、try/catch をつなげる。
floating promise のリスクを説明する。
commonMistakes: async/await は promise に基づかないと思う。
then callback から promise を return しない。
async work が終わる前に success を表示する。

sourceReferences.label: MDN Promise
sourceReferences.note: Used for promise states and chaining.

sourceReferences.label: MDN async function
sourceReferences.note: Used for async/await and promise relationship.
